§ 01

Component Timing NumbersThe accounting table — every critical path calc builds from these · Fig 4.11 / e4.5.4

"These timing numbers are not meant to be realistic today. If you want it to be realistic, we would not have nice single-digit numbers. Instruction memory is free — that's very typical. It could be a register."
02:16 ▶ Watch this moment Board — Shankar writes component timing values before tracing any path
📖 Figure e4.5.4 — Multicycle Datapath (multiData.pdf)

Each timing number Shankar assigns corresponds to a labeled component in Figure e4.5.4. When you trace a critical path, you're summing delays along a route through that figure. The fetch path hits InstrMem (free) → IR register (sequential, not counted in comb. path) → PC adder. The lw path is the longest because it traverses every major block.

ComponentTimingNotes
Register file read (RD1 / rs1,rs2)8 nsRead two source registers
Register file write (WD / rd)10 nsWrite result back
ALU (all ops)16 nsAdd, sub, and, or — all same
Data memory read/write25 nsArtificially slow; assume cache in practice
Memory data register delay (MD)5 nsFrom notes p5
MUX A / MUX B / WD MUX / PC-end MUX2 ns eachAll MUXes
Control signals5 nsAssigned but treated as free for path tracing
Instruction memory0 — freePre-clock-edge read — always free
These exact values appear on every critical path exam question. Know them cold: Reg-read=8, Reg-write=10, ALU=16, DataMem=25, MUX=2, Ctrl=5. Instruction memory is always free.
§ 02

Critical Path Analysis — Single-CycleLongest instruction sets clock rate · Textbook §4.4, Figure 4.21 (singleData.pdf)

In single-cycle: the clock period must cover the slowest instruction end to end. Method: a component's output stabilizes at max(all input stability times) + own_delay. Trace every parallel path and take max at each merge point.

"We figure out the time for every single instruction, see how long each takes. The clock cycle is the time for the longest instruction."
📖 Figure 4.21 — Complete Single-Cycle RISC-V Datapath (singleData.pdf)

Figure 4.21 shows the full datapath with all five major blocks labeled left to right: PC → InstrMem → RegFile → ALU → DataMem → RegFile. The lw critical path is the only instruction that activates all five blocks in series, which is why it's always the longest. Shankar traces this live using the color-coded paths: green = lw/sw, pink = beq, no color = R-type.

02:27 ▶ Watch this moment R-type path — Shankar traces stability times through MUX→ALU→RF
06:35 ▶ Watch this moment lw path — extra DataMem block makes it the critical path
R-type (add, sub, and…)
RF-read(8) → MUX-A(+2=10) → ALU(+16=26)
→ RF-write(+8=34) ns
max(34, PC+4≈3) = 38 ns
38 ns
lw (load word)
RF-read(8) → MUX-A(10) → ALU(26)
→ DataMem(+25=51 ns) ← longest!
Notes: max(8, 24+27) = 51
51 ns ← sets the clock
sw (store word) — 10%
RF-read → ALU → DataMem-write
max(8, 24+25) = 49 ns
(green dashed path in video)
49 ns
beq — 15%
RF-read → ALU-subtract → branch
No RF writeback → shorter
= 28 ns (pink path)
28 ns
13:04 ▶ Watch this moment CR = 1/51 ns ≈ 20 MHz derivation written on board
14:11 ▶ Watch this moment Competitor chip at 25 MHz — architect team must optimize lw path
Clock Rate = 1 / 51 ns = 1 / (51 × 10⁻⁹)
≈ 20 MHz ← this chip's spec
// To hit 25 MHz: period ≤ 40 ns → lw DataMem path must drop from 27 to <23 ns
Current single-cycle
20 MHz
Competitor target
25 MHz
After multicycle
31 MHz
Exam pattern: given a target clock rate, find which path must be optimized and by exactly how much. Always work backwards: target period = 1/target_freq, then required_reduction = current_path − target_period.
§ 03

Why Multicycle Is FasterInstruction mix argument · Wayin 16:58–20:14

"The multicycler is faster because 70% of the time it takes 42 nanoseconds, compared to 51 nanoseconds for single-cycle. While lw is slower, 85% of instructions run significantly faster."
The 14 ns clock cycle is an arbitrary design choice. A different cycle time shifts the tradeoff. This is the core of §1.6 performance analysis applied to §4.5 multicycle design.
InstructionMixCycles (CPI)Time @ 14 ns/cyclevs single-cycle (51 ns)
R-type70%33×14 = 42 ns ✓18% faster
lw10%4–54×14 = 56 ns ✗10% slower
sw5%3–442–56 nsneutral
beq15%2–328–42 ns ✓18–45% faster

70% + 15% = 85% of instructions run faster. lw (10%) is slightly slower but doesn't outweigh the gains. This justifies the multicycle design.

§ 04

Splitting Into Stages — Inter-Stage RegistersAdding clocked registers between combinational blocks · Fig e4.5.4 (multiData.pdf)

"To split up into stages, we need to introduce registers between stages. There's an instruction register — it stores the instruction obviously."
20:14 ▶ Watch this moment "Let's put this up in the stages. This is the multicycle design."
20:45 ▶ Watch this moment Registers IR, A, B, ALUOUT, MDR added between combinational blocks
📖 Figure e4.5.4 — Multicycle Datapath with Inter-Stage Registers (multiData.pdf)

Figure e4.5.4 (textbook supplement, also Figure 4.28 in main text) shows the multicycle datapath with IR, A, B, ALUOUT, and MDR inserted between the combinational stages. Hatched boxes = sequential (clocked) elements — this is Shankar's core drawing convention. The key insight: once a value is captured in a register at the clock edge, the next stage reads it in the following cycle.

RegisterCapturesWritten in CycleRead in Cycle
IRInstruction bits [31:0]① FetchAll subsequent cycles
PCProgram counter (already existed)① Fetch (PC←PC+4)Fetch, Decode
ARF[rs1] — first source② Decode③ Execute
BRF[rs2] — second source② Decode③ Execute or ④ Memory
ALUOUTALU result or address③ Execute④ Memory or ⑤ Writeback
MDRData read from DataRAM④ Memory (lw only)⑤ Writeback (lw only)
Hatched box = sequential element (clocked). Plain box/triangle = combinational. Shankar tests this convention directly on exams and homeworks. In e4.5.4, all the new inter-stage registers are hatched.
§ 05

Register Transfer Language (RTL)Formal notation for multicycle operations · Wayin 22:47+

"ADA, SystemC, Verilog — to automate. Important: number your cycles. [Will lose points if not numbered.] Left-hand side = a register. Right-hand side = function of registers."
NUMBER EVERY CYCLE. You will lose points if RTL statements are not labeled ①②③④⑤.
RuleExplanationExample
← arrowAssignment: register gets new value at clock edgePC ← PC + 4
Left sideAlways a register or memory write locationIR, DataRAM[ALUOUT]
Right sideFunction of register values at START of cycleRF[IR[19:15]], A + imm
Multiple per cycleSimultaneous — both happen in same clock cycleA ← RF[…]; B ← RF[…]
Unspecified signalsAssertible (read/write) = 0. Non-assertible (mux/opcode) = X—
Anticipatory readRead A and B in cycle ② before knowing instruction typeALUOUT ← PC+imm also computed
§ 06

RTL Cycles by InstructionComplete numbered RTL for R-type / lw / sw / beq + Fetch · Fig e4.5.12 (multiControl.pdf)

Shared — All Instructions (Cycles ① ②)
Fetch + Decode (universal)
① Fetch
State 0 — Instruction Fetch
IR ← InstrRAM[PC] // Fetch instruction into IR PC ← PC + 4 // Increment PC simultaneously
MemRead=1IorD=0 IRWrite=1ALUSrcA=0 ALUSrcB=01PCWrite=1 PCSource=0
② Decode
State 1 — Decode + Register Pre-Fetch (anticipatory)
A ← RF[IR[19:15]] // Read rs1 anticipatorily B ← RF[IR[24:20]] // Read rs2 anticipatorily ALUOUT ← PC + imm // Compute branch target in case it's beq
ALUSrcA=0 (PC)ALUSrcB=11 (imm)ALUOp=add
Decode is "trivial on RISC processor." Shankar combines it with register pre-fetch to avoid wasting a cycle. The key: read A and B before knowing the instruction — anticipatory reads.
R-type — 4 Cycles Total
24:22 ▶ Watch this moment Shankar numbers the R-type RTL cycles on board — check cycle labels
R-type — Cycles ① ② ③ ④
① ②
Same as shared fetch/decode above
③ Execute
"Look at what was produced last cycle, ask what we'll do with it"
ALUOUT ← A op B // op = add/sub/and/or from funct3/funct7
ALUSrcA=1 (A)ALUSrcB=00 (B)ALUOp=func
④ Writeback
"If it was on left, needs to be right next cycle"
RF[IR[11:7]] ← ALUOUT // Write result to rd (bits 11:7)
RegWrite=1MemtoReg=0
lw (load word) — 5 Cycles Total
27:29 ▶ Watch this moment Load word RTL — MDR step and RF writeback from MDR
lw — Cycles ① ② ③ ④ ⑤
① ②
Same as shared
③ Execute
ALUOUT ← A + immed// Compute effective address = rs1 + SignExt(imm12)
ALUSrcA=1ALUSrcB=10ALUOp=add
④ Memory
MDR ← DataRAM[ALUOUT]// Load word into Memory Data Register
MemRead=1IorD=1
⑤ Writeback
RF[IR[11:7]] ← MDR// Write loaded word to rd
RegWrite=1MemtoReg=1
sw (store word) — 4 Cycles Total
30:02 ▶ Watch this moment Store word RTL — B (rs2 data) written to computed address
sw — Cycles ① ② ③ ④
① ②
Same as shared
③ Execute
ALUOUT ← A + immed; B ← RF[IR[24:20]] // Compute store address; refresh B with rs2 data
ALUSrcA=1ALUSrcB=10ALUOp=add
④ Memory
DataMem[ALUOUT] ← B// Store rs2 value to computed address
MemWrite=1IorD=1
beq (branch equal) — 3 Cycles Total
33:05 ▶ Watch this moment Branch RTL — A−B=0 check, conditional PC←ALUOUT
beq — Cycles ① ② ③
① ②
Same — A,B contain rs1,rs2. ALUOUT already = branch target from ②.
③ Execute
if (A − B = 0) then PC ← ALUOUT // A−B via ALU subtract; if Zero flag → take branch to target already in ALUOUT
ALUSrcA=1ALUSrcB=00ALUOp=subPCWriteCond=1PCSource=1
From board: "Control: if not specified — read/write (assertible) = 0, mux/opcode (non-assertible) = X. DONE: Multicycle RISC-V for these instruction types."
Fetch Control Signals — State 0 (all instructions, every time)
36:20 ▶ Watch this moment Fetch cycle control signals — same for every instruction
State 0 (Fetch) — Complete Signal Set
State 0
// From notes (8:12 video): "memread = 1" is the key signal
MemRead=1IorD=0IRWrite=1 ALUSrcA=0 (PC)ALUSrcB=01 (const 4) ALUOp=addPCWrite=1 PCSource=0MemWrite=0RegWrite=0
Fetch signals are identical for ALL instructions. Any exam question "what signals are set in cycle 1?" always returns this exact set regardless of instruction type.
§ 07

Multicycle Performance AnalysisApr 23 content — CPI, clock rate, and MIPS

From Apr 23 notes. Clock rate ignoring the push instruction: 1/32 ns ≈ 31 MHz. Control path stability for State 1: max(25, 22) = 25 ns — this dictates the minimum clock cycle when the control logic is on the critical path.

// Mix: R-type=70%, lw=10%, sw=5%, beq=15% — CPI: R=4, lw=5, sw=4, beq=3
Avg CPI = (4×0.7) + (5×0.1) + (4×0.05) + (3×0.15)
= 2.8 + 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.45
= 3.95 CPI

Throughput = 31×10⁶ cycles/s ÷ 3.95 cycles/inst
≈ 7.8 MIPS
// Note from notes: "ends up being slower than single-cycle (lecture notes)" for this mix
// Unit cancellation from Ch.1: (cycles/s) ÷ (cycles/inst) = inst/s
Show all three steps: (1) avg CPI from mix, (2) clock rate, (3) MIPS = clock_rate / CPI. Shankar explicitly calls out the unit cancellation — include it.
§ 08

Textbook Figures — From Your GroupchatThree figures shared in class that map directly to this lecture

Figure C.5.1 — Microcode Field → Control Signal Mapping (Appendix C)
📖 Figure C.5.1 — P&H RISC-V 2nd Ed., Appendix C.5 "Translating a Microprogram to Hardware"

This table is the complete mapping from microprogram field names (like ALU control, SRC1, SRC2, Memory, Register control, PC write control, Sequencing) to the actual hardware control line values (ALUOp, ALUSrcA, ALUSrcB, MemRead/Write, RegWrite, MemtoReg, PCSource, etc.). Every signal name in Shankar's RTL cycles maps back to a row in this table.

For example: when Shankar writes ALUSrcA=1 in execute cycles, that corresponds to the SRC1 field value "A" in this table. When he writes MemtoReg=0 for R-type writeback, that's the "Write ALU" row under Register control. This table is the bridge between the RTL you write and the actual wires in the datapath hardware.

Figure e4.5.12 — Complete FSM for Multicycle Control (multiControl.pdf)
📖 Figure e4.5.12 / Figure 4.33 — Multicycle FSM (multiControl.pdf) — annotated with class RTL notes

This is the complete finite-state machine for the multicycle RISC-V control unit. Each circle = one clock cycle (one RTL step). The arc labels are opcode conditions that determine which state follows. States 0–1 are shared (Fetch, Decode). From State 2 onward the FSM branches per instruction type.

The annotation on the photo (bottom right) shows your cycle ② RTL: A←RF[IR[19:15]]; B←RF[IR[24:20]]; ALUOUT←PC+imm; Execute — this is State 1 in the FSM. The left-side annotation shows the control signals added for the sw/push-style state: MemWrite, IorD=1, ALUSrcA=10, ALUSrcB=1, ALUOP=ADD.

HW4 required adding new states to this FSM for jalr and stw. The new states branch off from State 2 (memory address computation) using new opcode condition arcs.

§ 10

Homework ContextHW4 — Multicycle RISC-V (B) — stw instruction

HW4 Part B — stw (store word twice) alias: Etzioni (classmate reference)

How it connects to this lecture: HW4B asks you to design a new instruction — stw regx, offset(regy) — that stores regx to both offset(regy) and offset+4(regy). This requires exactly the skills Shankar taught in §06: writing numbered RTL cycles using ALUOUT as the address register, chaining DataMem writes across cycles, and re-using ALUOUT in a subsequent cycle (ALUOUT ← ALUOUT+4).

The critical insight this lecture gives you: ALUOUT is a register that persists across clock cycles. The classmate solution below uses this to execute two memory writes in cycles ④ and ⑤ by incrementing ALUOUT between them — exactly the pattern Shankar demonstrated for sw (§06) and the push example (§09 p.4).

Which FSM state to add: From Figure e4.5.12 (§08), you branch from State 2 with a new opcode arc, add states for the two DataMem writes, and return to State 0. The control signals for each write state follow the "Write ALU" row of Figure C.5.1 (§08): MemWrite=1, IorD=1.

Classmate Reference — HW4(B) RTL Solution
This is a peer submission shared in the class groupchat. Use it to check your approach and understanding, not to copy.
What it shows: stw machine code (S-type layout: imm[11:5] | regx | regy | funct3 | imm[4:0] | opcode). RTL: ① Fetch, ② Decode (A←rf[rs1], B←rf[rs2], ALUOUT←PC+imm), ③ ALUOUT←A+imm (offset), ④ DataMem[ALUOUT]←B; ALUOUT←ALUOUT+4, ⑤ DataMem[ALUOUT]←B. The key move: reuse ALUOUT in the same cycle ④ for both writing and incrementing.
Section Map
§01 Component Timings ....... Reg-read=8, Reg-write=10, ALU=16, DataMem=25, MUX=2
§02 Critical Path ........... R=38, lw=51 (critical), sw=49, beq=28 → CR=20MHz
§03 Multicycle Speed ........ 85% faster; 3.95 CPI
§04 Stage Registers ......... IR, A, B, ALUOUT, MDR; hatched=sequential
§05 RTL Basics .............. Number every cycle. Left←right. Assertible=0, X otherwise.
§06 RTL by Instruction ...... ①② shared; R=4c, lw=5c, sw=4c, beq=3c
§07 Performance ............. CR=31MHz; CPI=3.95; 7.8 MIPS
§08 Figures ................. Fig C.5.1 (microcode→signals) + Fig e4.5.12 (FSM)
§09 Your Notes .............. All 5 pages transcribed
§10 HW Context .............. HW4B stw — applies ALUOUT persistence + two DataMem writes
